title: First MacBook Neo Benchmarks Are In: Here's How It Compares to the M1 MacBook Air
content:
Benchmarks for the new MacBook Neo surfaced today, and unsurprisingly, CPU performance is almost identical to the iPhone 16 Pro. The ‚ÄåMacBook Neo‚Äå uses the same 6-core A18 Pro chip that was first introduced in the iPhone 16 Pro, but it has one fewer GPU core.


The ‚ÄåMacBook Neo‚Äå earned a single-core score of 3461 and a multi-core score of 8668, along with a Metal score of 31286.

Here's how the Neo's scores compare to ‚ÄåiPhone 16‚Äå Pro and other devices that make apt comparisons:

  • iPhone 16 Pro - 3445 single-core, 8624 multi-core, 32575 Metal

  • M1 MacBook Air - 2346 single-core, 8342 multi-core, 33148 Metal

  • M4 MacBook Air - 3696 single-core, 14730 multi-core, 54630 Metal

  • M3 iPad Air - 3048 single-core, 11678 multi-core, 44395 Metal

  • iPad 11 - 2587 single-core, 6036 multi-core, 19395 Metal


Since the ‚ÄåMacBook Neo‚Äå has one fewer GPU core than the ‚ÄåiPhone 16‚Äå Pro, it makes sense to see a slightly lower Metal score.

When comparing the ‚ÄåMacBook Neo‚Äå's performance to existing Macs, the A18 Pro's multi-core performance is on par with the M1 chip in the MacBook Air, but single-core performance is much higher than it was with the ‚ÄåM1‚Äå. It's closer to the M3 or M4 chip.

High single-core performance is a good fit for the ‚ÄåMacBook Neo‚Äå audience, because single-core speeds are important for activities like web browsing, using document apps, and streaming video. The ‚ÄåMacBook Neo‚Äå isn't really built for video editing, music creation, 3D modeling, and similar tasks where multi-core performance makes more of a difference.

Apple does not compare ‚ÄåMacBook Neo‚Äå performance to other Macs, iPads, or iPhones. The company's low-cost MacBook focuses on competing with similar-priced Windows PCs and Chromebooks. In its marketing materials, Apple says the A18 Pro is up to 50 percent faster for "everyday tasks" than the bestselling PC with the latest shipping Intel Core Ultra 5. It's also up to 3x faster for on-device AI workloads and up to 2x faster for photo editing.

We're only seeing one benchmark result so far, so averages could be slightly different when more Neo machines are benchmarked, but the scores are right in line with what's expected.

The ‚ÄåMacBook Neo‚Äå is priced starting at $599, and it is available for preorder now. It is set to launch on March 11.
